generated_comment: Honestly in most homes it works better when each person handles what they are naturally better at. I take care of finances, bills, outside work and driving, while my wife manages the kitchen, kids' routine and overall home coordination much more efficiently than I ever could. She also works, but that doesn't mean every role has to become 50-50 on paper—practical division is not sexism, and when husbands make tea once in a while people act like wow, what a modern man.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it naturalizes a gendered division of labor as 'practical' and assumes men are better suited for external/financial tasks while women should manage domestic work. It encodes efficiency framing, 'he handles finances, she handles home,' and the low-bar celebration of minimal male contribution.
original_frame: Marital Rape: A Husband's Right, A Wife's Duty
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 36. Married. A man in Mumbai. You are commenting under your real name on a Facebook post where someone has shared an article about marital rape legislation. The comment section is divided. You are writing a detailed comment explaining why you think criminalising marital rape will destroy Indian marriages, why consent within marriage is a western concept that does not map onto Indian family structures, and what you think wives who use this law will use it for.
